column1: Copied from the latest tailored AI Readiness content pack.

column4: finding_title
column5: finding_summary
column6: business_impact

column4: AI intent is trend-led rather than business-led
column5: Leadership interest in AI is present, but the business has not yet translated that interest into a defined commercial thesis, an owned problem statement, or a ranked set of use cases.
column6: Investment decisions are likely to be reactive, and resources may be spent on tools before priority business problems are agreed.

column4: AI direction exists but portfolio discipline is still weak
column5: The business has started to define where AI may help, but ownership, ranking logic, and sequencing are not yet strong enough to govern investment confidently.
column6: Promising ideas may compete for attention without a clear basis for selecting the best pilots first.

column4: AI strategy is commercially grounded and governable
column5: The business has articulated why AI matters, which use cases come first, who owns decisions, and how priorities link back to business outcomes.
column6: The organisation is in a stronger position to approve pilots based on value, readiness, and execution discipline rather than hype.

column4: Candidate workflows are not yet ready for credible AI selection
column5: The business has not yet narrowed AI down to clearly bounded workflows with defined pain points, stable patterns, and operational review design.
column6: AI may be pointed at the wrong work, or introduced into processes that still need process mapping and stabilisation first.

column4: Use case candidates are visible but workflow design still needs tightening
column5: Several workflows appear promising for AI, but workflow boundaries, root pain points, or human-in-the-loop controls are not yet complete enough for confident pilot design.
column6: Pilot quality and adoption may suffer because the workflow operating model is not fully locked down.

column4: High-fit workflows have been identified with practical pilot potential
column5: The business has isolated workflows with clear pain, repeatable patterns, meaningful volume, and tasks that genuinely suit AI support such as drafting, classification, retrieval, or summarisation.
column6: Pilot selection can focus on workflows where AI has a stronger chance of improving speed, quality, or consistency.

column4: Priority use cases are constrained by weak data foundations
column5: The required data for the shortlisted AI use cases is fragmented, inconsistently defined, poor in quality, weakly owned, or difficult to retrieve in a controlled way.
column6: AI outputs will be unreliable, difficult to trust, or expensive to operationalise until the data foundation is improved.

column4: Data is usable in parts, but not yet consistently deployment-ready
column5: The business has enough data to explore selected use cases, but important gaps remain in ownership, structure, access, history, or knowledge organisation.
column6: AI pilots may work in controlled scope, but scaling will be limited unless the supporting data foundation is tightened.

column4: Data foundations are sufficiently strong for priority AI use cases
column5: The main structured and unstructured inputs needed for the chosen use cases exist, are accessible, and are governed well enough to support controlled deployment.
column6: The business can move toward pilot design with greater confidence in output quality, retrieval quality, and operational trust.

column4: System fragmentation will restrict operational AI value
column5: The application landscape is not yet cleanly documented or connected enough to move AI outputs into the live workflow without manual workarounds, fragile handoffs, or support risk.
column6: Even well-chosen use cases may fail to create business value because outputs remain disconnected from the systems where teams actually work.

column4: There is a technical path, but workflow embedding is not yet strong enough
column5: Relevant systems can support parts of the AI solution, but manual transfers, incomplete dependency mapping, or limited insertion points still weaken the path from pilot to production.
column6: A pilot may run, but operational adoption and scale will be harder unless integration design is strengthened.

column4: The technology environment can support embedded AI delivery
column5: The core systems, integration methods, ownership, and workflow insertion points are sufficiently clear to place AI outputs inside real operational processes.
column6: The business can pursue pilots that work inside existing systems rather than adding another disconnected tool.

column4: Process discipline is not yet strong enough for AI layering
column5: Target workflows remain unstable, inconsistently executed, dependent on tribal knowledge, or crowded with unaddressed rule-based tasks that should be fixed before AI is introduced.
column6: AI would add complexity to weak processes and is likely to amplify inconsistency instead of reducing it.

column4: The process base is improving, but selected workflows still need control tightening
column5: Core workflows have some repeatability, but variation, unclear exceptions, partial SOP coverage, or weak automation discipline still reduce AI readiness.
column6: AI may be viable in limited areas, but process maturity gaps will still constrain quality, scalability, and reviewer burden.

column4: Processes are mature enough to support controlled AI augmentation
column5: The priority workflows are sufficiently documented, repeatable, and operationally controlled to support AI assistance without destabilising the process.
column6: The business can introduce AI into workflows where process variation and oversight burden are less likely to undermine results.

column4: AI control requirements are not yet strong enough for safe deployment
column5: Rules for approved data use, sensitive data handling, human approval, traceability, vendor review, and governance ownership are incomplete or weak for the intended AI scenarios.
column6: The organisation is exposed to avoidable privacy, security, compliance, and accountability risk if deployment moves ahead too quickly.

column4: Basic AI controls exist, but operating governance is still incomplete
column5: The business has started to define acceptable use and control expectations, but auditability, risk ownership, legal interpretation, or approval logic still need strengthening for live deployment.
column6: Low-risk pilots may be possible, but governance gaps increase exposure as use cases become more material or externally facing.

column4: Governance foundations can support responsible AI use
column5: The business has workable policy, review, audit, access, and oversight structures for the selected AI scenarios.
column6: AI can be introduced with clearer control boundaries, accountability, and decision rights.

column4: Adoption risk is likely to undermine implementation
column5: Leadership, managers, or users do not yet show enough practical understanding, trust, ownership, or change readiness to absorb AI into the way work is actually done.
column6: Even a technically sound deployment could underperform because teams may resist it, misuse it, or fail to change the workflow around it.

column4: Readiness to adopt AI is visible but still uneven
column5: There is interest and some momentum for AI, but training, manager readiness, oversight clarity, or ownership of adoption still need to mature.
column6: Pilots may gain traction, but sustained usage and operating change are not yet guaranteed.

column4: The business has the people conditions needed for adoption
column5: Leaders understand the practical role of AI, managers are ready to adapt workflows, users are reasonably prepared, and adoption ownership is visible.
column6: The organisation is better placed to move from pilot launch to actual usage and workflow change.

column4: The business cannot yet prove AI value with confidence
column5: Baseline measures, success definitions, pilot scorecards, financial value logic, or post-launch accountability are not strong enough to evaluate AI as an investment.
column6: AI may be funded as an experiment, but the business will struggle to know whether to scale, redesign, or stop.

column4: Measurement logic exists, but not yet at decision-grade strength
column5: Some value measures and pilot KPIs are visible, but comparisons, scale thresholds, or financial assumptions still need to be tightened.
column6: AI results may look promising, but scaling decisions will still rely partly on judgment rather than evidence.

column4: AI value can be measured, governed, and used for scale decisions
column5: The business has clear baselines, success metrics, comparison logic, and financial framing for the priority use cases.
column6: Leadership can make stronger go, pause, redesign, or scale decisions based on evidence rather than enthusiasm.
